input_candidates.truth_value: Investigates the isothermal oxidation resistance of high purity Fe-Mn-Al alloys at 600–1000°C, producing an oxide map at 800°C and determining the influence of Mn and Al on scale morphology and protective alumina formation.

input_candidates.truth_value: Quaternary alloy duplex α+γ, oxidized at 800°C 24 hr: highly convoluted scale detached from substrate; substrate surface became fully ferritic and Mn‑depleted; scale base enriched in Al; thicker sections enriched in Mn.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fe-Mn-Al-Cr alloys show varied scale morphologies: thin protective alumina on optimized compositions; hollow convoluted oxide mounds or wrinkled scales on low-Al or high-Mn compositions; duplex alloys form scales above austenite.

input_candidates.truth_value: Region IV alloy (high Al, >9%): alumina scale penetrated by numerous small manganese‑rich oxide nodules. Smooth rounded voids beneath spalled alumina. Nodule growth involves Mn vapor transport.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Region IV alloys with >9% Al forming alumina scales penetrated by small manganese-rich oxide nodules; voids present beneath spalled areas.

input_candidates.truth_value: Oxide map (Fig. 3) at 800°C showing four composition regions based on scale morphology: Region I (low Al) – alumina with large/small oxide nodules; Region II (duplex α+γ) – alumina with bulky Mn‑rich scales and oxide threads; Region III (ferritic, optimum 5–10% Mn, 6–10% Al) – continuous protective alumina with small protrusions; Region IV (Al >9%) – alumina with small Mn‑rich nodules and spallation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Kinetics: Log-log plots of weight gain versus time (Fig. 1) show breakaway and healing behavior for non-protective alloys and near-parabolic behavior for protective alumina formers. The rate equation (ΔW/A)^n = kt yielded a large range of n values, indicating few alloys obeyed simple rate equations; ΔW/24 values were used for comparison of oxidation rates.

input_candidates.truth_value: Manganese addition: <2% Mn drastically increases oxidation rate; 5–10% Mn reduces rate by replacing large iron oxide nodules with smaller Mn‑rich nodules; >10% Mn promotes austenite formation and bulky manganese oxides that degrade oxidation resistance.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Table I (24 hr at 800°C): Weight gains of Fe-Mn-Al alloys span from approximately 0.021 mg cm^-2 (Fe-9Mn-4Al) to over 1 mg cm^-2 (Fe-20Mn-6Al, 18 hr). Minimum weight gains occur in the composition range Fe-(5–10)%Mn-(6–10)%Al (e.g., Fe-5Mn-7Al: 0.033; Fe-10Mn-6Al: 0.054; Fe-5Mn-9Al: 0.021 mg cm^-2). Additions of less than 2% Mn greatly increased oxidation rate of binary Fe-Al alloys, whereas 5–10% Mn significantly reduced it.

input_candidates.truth_value: Austenite is detrimental: it promotes breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and growth of bulky Mn‑rich oxides. Totally ferritic alloys necessary for good oxidation resistance.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Table II (100 hr): Fe-10Mn-6Al weight gains: 0.171 (600°C), 0.117 (800°C), 0.450 (1000°C) mg cm^-2. Fe-20Mn-8Al: 0.101 (600°C), 0.242 (800°C, unreliable due to spalling), 0.253 (1000°C, unreliable due to spalling) mg cm^-2.

input_candidates.truth_value: Small chromium additions (e.g., 12% Cr in Fe‑10Mn‑4Al‑12Cr) enable protective alumina scale formation at lower Al contents. Alloys with less than 5% Al require high Cr to avoid breakaway.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Table III (Fe-Mn-Al-Cr at 800°C): Fe-10Mn-4Al-12Cr showed lowest 24h weight gain 0.061 mg cm^-2 among Cr alloys; Fe-5Mn-5Al-5Cr: 0.068; Fe-7.5Mn-5Al-5Cr: 0.063. Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr showed highest 24h gain 2.663 mg cm^-2. Fe-5Mn-4Al-5Cr data 0.292 mg cm^-2 corresponds to 15 hr oxidation.

input_candidates.truth_value: At 600°C higher Al levels are needed to form protective scales; the α/(α+γ) phase boundary shifts, increasing austenite fraction in some alloys (e.g., Fe‑20Mn‑8Al). At 1000°C both test alloys were ferritic and formed convoluted alumina scales.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Oxide map (Fig. 3) at 800°C classifies alloys into four regions superimposed on the phase diagram: Region I (insufficient Al, alumina + bulky nodules), Region II (duplex α+γ, alumina + bulky Mn-rich oxides + threads), Region III (fully ferritic, continuous protective alumina, Fe-(5–10)%Mn-(6–10)%Al), Region IV (>9% Al, alumina + small penetrating Mn oxide nodules).

input_candidates.truth_value: Nodule growth mechanism in Region IV involves void formation beneath alumina scale, Mn vapor transport through voids, reaction with oxygen to form Mn2O3, and subsequent filling of voids with Mn oxide followed by Al healing layer.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 17 schematic of nodule structures at 800°C: Type 1 (large iron oxide nodules, <5% Mn) has Fe2O3 outer, (Fe,Mn)3O4 inner, discontinuous Al2O3/aluminate. Type 2 (5–7.5% Mn) similar with partial healing layer. Type 3 (≥7.5% Mn) small Mn-rich nodules with continuous Mn2O3 outer layer and complete healing layer of Al2O3/aluminate at base; absence of (Fe,Mn)3O4 due to continuous aluminate.

input_candidates.truth_value: Healing layers of Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 at the metal‑oxide interface can stop growth of large nodules and cause transition to protective behavior. Incomplete isolation of (Fe,Mn)3O4 layer leads to enhanced Fe2O3 formation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 18 schematic of duplex alloy oxidation stages: (1) alumina forms over ferrite matrix, (2) austenite nucleates and grows beneath alumina, (3) Mn2O3 threads grow at weaknesses in alumina, (4) threads incorporated into bulky Mn-rich oxide with Mn depletion and austenite conversion back to ferrite, (5) continuous healing layer of Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 forms.
